title: Minimum deletions to make array sorted.
description: Minimum deletions to make array sorted.

examples.explanation: Array: [3,2,1], n=3. Start with idx=0, last=-inf, len=0. At idx=0 (3): Option 1: Include 3 (valid since 3 > -inf), recurse with idx=1, last=3, len=1. At idx=1 (2): Include 2 invalid (2 < 3), so only exclude path. Recurse with idx=2, last=3, len=1. At idx=2 (1): Include 1 invalid (1 < 3), exclude. Recurse with idx=3, last=3, len=1 (base case). Subsequence [3] sorted, max_len=1. Option 2: Exclude 3, recurse with idx=1, last=-inf, len=0. At idx=1 (2): Include 2 valid, recurse idx=2, last=2, len=1. At idx=2 (1): Include 1 invalid (1 < 2), exclude. Recurse idx=3, last=2, len=1. Subsequence [2] sorted, max_len=1. Exclude 2 path: recurse idx=2, last=-inf, len=0. At idx=2 (1): Include 1 valid, recurse idx=3, last=1, len=1. Subsequence [1] sorted, max_len=1. Exclude 1 path: recurse idx=3, last=-inf, len=0. Empty subsequence sorted, max_len=0. Overall max_len=1. Minimum deletions = 3 - 1 = 2.

Array: [1,2,3,4], n=4. Start idx=0, last=-inf, len=0. Recursively explore all subsequences. The sorted subsequence [1,2,3,4] will be found when including all elements in order (each element > previous). This gives len=4. Other subsequences like [1,2,3], [1,2,4], etc., have lengths ≤4. Maximum length found is 4. Minimum deletions = 4 - 4 = 0.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsequences of the array recursively, check if each is sorted, and find the longest sorted subsequence. The minimum deletions is the array length minus the length of the longest sorted subsequence. This is straightforward but highly inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Deletions to Make Array Sorted via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution using brute force recursion to find minimum deletions by exploring all subsequences.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that to make an array sorted with minimum deletions, we need to keep as many elements as possible while maintaining sorted order. This is equivalent to finding the longest sorted subsequence (LSS) in the array. The minimum deletions equals the array length minus the length of the LSS. We use brute force recursion to generate all possible subsequences (2^n possibilities) by recursively including or excluding each element. For each generated subsequence, we check if it's sorted. We track the maximum length of sorted subsequences found. The algorithm starts with an empty subsequence and processes each element from index 0 to n-1. At each step, we have two choices: include the current element (if it maintains sorted order) or exclude it. We recursively explore both paths. The base case is when we've processed all elements, at which point we check if the current subsequence is sorted and update the maximum length if needed. Time complexity is O(2^n) because we generate all subsequences. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and the current subsequence storage. This approach is straightforward but highly inefficient for n > 20 due to exponential growth. Connection to the problem: This directly solves the minimum deletions problem by finding the complement of the LSS. Common mistakes include forgetting to check sorted condition when including elements, not properly backtracking, or having incorrect base case handling. Real code pattern: Use recursion with parameters for current index, last included element value, and current subsequence length.

examples.trace: Array: [3,2,1], n=3. Start with idx=0, last=-inf, len=0. At idx=0 (3): Option 1: Include 3 (valid since 3 > -inf), recurse with idx=1, last=3, len=1. At idx=1 (2): Include 2 invalid (2 < 3), so only exclude path. Recurse with idx=2, last=3, len=1. At idx=2 (1): Include 1 invalid (1 < 3), exclude. Recurse with idx=3, last=3, len=1 (base case). Subsequence [3] sorted, max_len=1. Option 2: Exclude 3, recurse with idx=1, last=-inf, len=0. At idx=1 (2): Include 2 valid, recurse idx=2, last=2, len=1. At idx=2 (1): Include 1 invalid (1 < 2), exclude. Recurse idx=3, last=2, len=1. Subsequence [2] sorted, max_len=1. Exclude 2 path: recurse idx=2, last=-inf, len=0. At idx=2 (1): Include 1 valid, recurse idx=3, last=1, len=1. Subsequence [1] sorted, max_len=1. Exclude 1 path: recurse idx=3, last=-inf, len=0. Empty subsequence sorted, max_len=0. Overall max_len=1. Minimum deletions = 3 - 1 = 2.

Array: [1,2,3,4], n=4. Start idx=0, last=-inf, len=0. Recursively explore all subsequences. The sorted subsequence [1,2,3,4] will be found when including all elements in order (each element > previous). This gives len=4. Other subsequences like [1,2,3], [1,2,4], etc., have lengths ≤4. Maximum length found is 4. Minimum deletions = 4 - 4 = 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Generation of All Sorted Subsequences
ladders.desc: Recursive function to generate all sorted subsequences and track the longest one.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach. The intuition is to systematically explore all possible subsequences while maintaining sorted order constraints. We use depth-first recursion where at each array index, we decide whether to include the current element in our subsequence. The key constraint: we can only include an element if it's greater than or equal to the last included element (to maintain non-decreasing order). The algorithm takes parameters: current index i, last included value last_val, and current subsequence length curr_len. Base case: if i == n (end of array), we've completed a subsequence; we update max_len if curr_len > max_len. Recursive case: First, try excluding the current element: recurse with i+1, last_val unchanged, curr_len unchanged. Second, try including the current element if arr[i] >= last_val: recurse with i+1, last_val = arr[i], curr_len+1. We initialize with i=0, last_val = -infinity (so first element always includable), curr_len=0. Time complexity: O(2^n) worst-case as we explore all inclusion/exclusion combinations. Space complexity: O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function directly computes the longest sorted subsequence length, which L0 uses to calculate minimum deletions. Common mistakes: Using > instead of >= for sorted check (problem allows equal elements), not using -infinity for initial last_val, or not exploring exclude path. Real code pattern: Recursive function with backtracking-style exploration.

examples.trace: Array [2,1], n=2. Start: i=0, last=-inf, len=0. At i=0 (2): Include: recurse i=1, last=2, len=1. At i=1 (1): Include invalid (1 < 2), exclude: recurse i=2, last=2, len=1 (base). Subsequence [2] len=1. Exclude at i=0: recurse i=1, last=-inf, len=0. At i=1 (1): Include: recurse i=2, last=1, len=1. Subsequence [1] len=1. Exclude: recurse i=2, last=-inf, len=0. Empty len=0. Max len found = 1. But wait, we missed [2,1] is not sorted. Correct max_len=1. Actually for [2,1], longest sorted subsequence is either [2] or [1], length 1. Output is length 1. Let me correct: The function returns max_len=1. Example should show process. Trace: All paths: (include 2, exclude 1) -> [2] len1; (exclude 2, include 1) -> [1] len1; (exclude both) -> [] len0. Max=1.

Array [1,2,1], n=3. Start i=0, last=-inf, len=0. Recursively explore: Path1: include 1 (i=0), include 2 (i=1, 2>=1), include 1 (i=2, 1>=2 invalid), so exclude at i=2 -> subsequence [1,2] len2. Path2: include 1, exclude 2, include 1 -> [1,1] len2. Path3: exclude 1, include 2, include 1 invalid -> [2] len1. Many other paths. Maximum length found is 2 (e.g., [1,2] or [1,1]).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Inclusion/Exclusion Decision with Sorted Constraint
ladders.desc: At each element, decide to include (if maintains sorted order) or exclude, and recurse.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the decision-making process at each recursive step. Intuition: To build a sorted subsequence, we process the array left to right. For each element arr[i], we have two choices: 1) Include it in the subsequence, but only if it doesn't break the sorted order (i.e., arr[i] >= last included value). 2) Exclude it, which is always allowed. This creates a binary decision tree where each path represents a subsequence. The algorithm ensures we explore all possible subsequences while respecting the sorted constraint. Step-by-step: At index i, with last value L and current length len: Check if i == n (base case). If not, first make recursive call for exclude: recurse(i+1, L, len). Then check if arr[i] >= L: if true, make recursive call for include: recurse(i+1, arr[i], len+1). This order doesn't matter. The sorted constraint is crucial: without it, we'd generate all subsequences but many would be unsorted. Time per recursive call is O(1), but total calls O(2^n). Space O(n). Connection to L1: This logic is the recursive body of the L1 function. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check i == n base case first, incorrect comparison (e.g., > instead of >=), or not propagating last value correctly. Real code pattern: Simple if-else with recursive calls.

examples.trace: At i=0, arr[0]=5. Since 5 >= -inf, we can include. First, exclude call: recurse(i=1, last=-inf, len=0). Second, include call: recurse(i=1, last=5, len=1). So two branches created.
At i=1, arr[1]=1. Check if 1 >= 5? No. So include not allowed. Only exclude call: recurse(i=2, last=5, len=1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking if a Subsequence is Sorted
ladders.desc: Function to verify if a given sequence is non-decreasing.
ladders.explanation: This building block checks whether a sequence (array or list) is sorted in non-decreasing order. Intuition: A sequence is sorted if each element is less than or equal to the next element. We need to verify this property for any generated subsequence. The algorithm iterates through the sequence from index 0 to len-2, comparing each element with the next. If at any point sequence[i] > sequence[i+1], the sequence is not sorted. If we complete the loop without finding such a pair, the sequence is sorted. For empty or single-element sequences, they are trivially sorted. Step-by-step: Input: array A of length m. If m <= 1, return true. For i from 0 to m-2: if A[i] > A[i+1], return false. After loop, return true. Time complexity: O(m) where m is subsequence length. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This check is used implicitly in L2 via the constraint arr[i] >= last; if we build subsequence incrementally, we ensure sortedness without explicit full check. But in base case of L1, we might check the complete subsequence (though in our implementation we maintain sortedness incrementally). Common mistakes: Using >= instead of > for unsorted check (equal elements are allowed), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, or not handling empty case. Real code pattern: Simple for-loop with comparison.

examples.trace: Sequence length 3. Check i=0: 1 <= 2, ok. i=1: 2 <= 3, ok. Loop completes, return true.
Sequence length 3. Check i=0: 3 > 1, so return false immediately.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Elements for Sorted Order
ladders.desc: Compare two numbers to determine if they are in non-decreasing order.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation used throughout the algorithm: comparing two elements to check if they maintain sorted order. Intuition: In a sorted (non-decreasing) sequence, each element must be less than or equal to the next element. Therefore, for two elements a and b, the condition a <= b must hold for them to be in sorted order. If a > b, then they are out of order. The operation is simple: given a and b, return true if a <= b, false otherwise. This is used in two contexts: 1) When building subsequences incrementally (L2), we compare current element arr[i] with last included value L to decide if we can include arr[i]. 2) When checking a full subsequence (L3), we compare consecutive pairs. Step-by-step: Input a, b. Compute a <= b. If true, elements are in order; if false, they are out of order. Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is repeated in the loop of L3 to check entire sequence. Common mistakes: Using < instead of <= (which would disallow equal elements), or comparing in wrong order. Real code pattern: Simple boolean expression.

examples.trace: 2 <= 5 is true, so in order.
5 <= 2 is false, so out of order.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsequences
ladders.desc: A subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some elements without changing order.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: what is a subsequence? Intuition: Given an array or sequence, a subsequence is obtained by selecting some elements (possibly none or all) while maintaining their original relative order. You cannot reorder elements; you can only choose to include or exclude each element. For example, from array [A, B, C], possible subsequences include: [] (empty), [A], [B], [C], [A,B], [A,C], [B,C], [A,B,C]. Note that [B,A] is NOT a subsequence because order is changed. The number of subsequences of an array of length n is 2^n (including empty), because each element has 2 choices: include or exclude. Step-by-step to generate: Consider each element one by one, decide include/exclude. Connection to L4: When we compare elements for sorted order in L4, we are ensuring that in a subsequence, the included elements are in order. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring (which requires consecutive elements) or with permutations (which allow reordering). Real code pattern: Concept only, but often represented by binary inclusion vectors.

examples.trace: For element 1: include or exclude. For element 2: include or exclude. Combine: exclude both -> []. include 1, exclude 2 -> [1]. exclude 1, include 2 -> [2]. include both -> [1,2]. Total 4 subsequences.
Same logic: 2 elements, 2^2=4 subsequences. Maintain order: [X,Y] is valid, [Y,X] is not.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming LIS O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Compute the length of the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) using a DP array where dp[i] stores the LIS ending at index i. Iterate over all previous indices to update dp[i] based on sorted conditions. Minimum deletions is array length minus the maximum value in dp.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Deletions to Make Array Sorted using DP LIS O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution: compute minimum deletions by finding longest increasing subsequence length.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to minimize deletions, we should keep as many elements as possible while ensuring the remaining sequence is sorted. This is equivalent to finding the longest increasing subsequence (LIS) in the array. The minimum deletions equals the total array length minus the LIS length. We use dynamic programming with an array dp where dp[i] stores the length of the LIS ending at index i. For each element at index i, we check all previous indices j (0 ≤ j < i) and if nums[j] ≤ nums[i] (for non-decreasing order), we update dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). The maximum value in dp gives the LIS length. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops, and space is O(n) for the dp array. This connects to the key subroutine of computing LIS length. Common mistakes include using strict inequality (< instead of ≤) for non-decreasing order, not initializing dp to 1, or incorrectly computing deletions. Real code pattern: def min_deletions(nums): n = len(nums); dp = [1]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if nums[j] <= nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1); return n - max(dp).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Longest Increasing Subsequence Length O(n²)
ladders.desc: Find the length of the longest increasing subsequence in an array.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to find the longest subsequence where elements are in non-decreasing order, which is a classic dynamic programming problem. We define dp[i] as the length of the LIS ending at index i. For each element, we look at all previous elements; if a previous element is less than or equal to the current, we can extend that subsequence. The recurrence is dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1) for all j < i where nums[j] ≤ nums[i]. Initialize all dp[i] to 1 since each element alone is a subsequence of length 1. The LIS length is the maximum value in dp. Time complexity is O(n²) from nested loops, and space is O(n). This is the core of the full problem, as deletions = n - LIS length. Common mistakes include using strict < instead of ≤, or not considering all j. Real code pattern: def lis_length(nums): n = len(nums); dp = [1]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if nums[j] <= nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j]+1); return max(dp).

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence for LIS Ending at Each Index
ladders.desc: Compute dp[i] as max LIS length ending at index i using previous indices.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to find the LIS ending at index i, we consider all previous indices j where the element is less than or equal to nums[i], and extend their subsequences. Formally, dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) for all j < i with nums[j] ≤ nums[i], or 1 if no such j exists. This works because the LIS ending at i must include nums[i] and can be built by appending nums[i] to the LIS ending at some j. We iterate i from 0 to n-1, and for each i, iterate j from 0 to i-1 to update dp[i]. Time complexity for computing one dp[i] is O(i), leading to O(n²) total, and space is O(n) for dp. This is the building block for the LIS length computation. Common mistakes include not initializing dp[i] to 1, or incorrectly handling the max. Real code pattern: def compute_dp(nums): n = len(nums); dp = [1]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if nums[j] <= nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1); return dp.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Over Previous Indices to Update Current DP Value
ladders.desc: For a given index i, check all j < i to find the best previous subsequence to extend.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to determine the best LIS ending at i, we need to examine all elements before i to see if we can append nums[i] to their subsequences. This involves a nested loop where the outer loop iterates i from 0 to n-1, and the inner loop iterates j from 0 to i-1. For each j, if nums[j] ≤ nums[i], we consider extending the subsequence ending at j by including nums[i], which would give length dp[j] + 1. We take the maximum over all such j to set dp[i]. If no j satisfies the condition, dp[i] remains 1. This step is O(i) time for each i, contributing to the overall O(n²) complexity. It connects to the core logic by implementing the recurrence. Common mistakes include skipping j=0 or using wrong comparison. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if nums[j] <= nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1).

examples.trace: Given nums=[5,2,3,6,1], current dp=[1,1,2,1,1] (after processing i=0,1,2). For i=3, nums[3]=6. Initialize dp[3]=1. Check j=0: nums[0]=5 ≤ 6, so dp[3]=max(1, dp[0]+1=2)=2. j=1: nums[1]=2 ≤ 6, dp[3]=max(2, dp[1]+1=2)=2. j=2: nums[2]=3 ≤ 6, dp[3]=max(2, dp[2]+1=3)=3. No more j. Final dp[3]=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Update DP Value Based on Condition
ladders.desc: Check if a previous element is ≤ current element and update dp[i] accordingly.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for a fixed i and j, we need to decide whether nums[j] can come before nums[i] in a sorted subsequence. If nums[j] ≤ nums[i], then we can extend the subsequence ending at j by including nums[i], resulting in a potential new length of dp[j] + 1. We compare this with the current dp[i] and update dp[i] to the maximum value. This is a fundamental operation in dynamic programming for LIS. It involves a comparison (nums[j] ≤ nums[i]) and an update (dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1)). Time complexity is O(1) per pair (i,j), and space is O(1). This feeds into the building block by being the core step in the inner loop. Common mistakes include using < instead of ≤, or not adding 1 to dp[j]. Real code pattern: if nums[j] <= nums[i]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1).

examples.trace: Given nums=[5,2,3,6,1], dp=[1,1,1,1,1]. For i=2, j=1: nums[1]=2, nums[2]=3. Check if 2 ≤ 3: yes. Current dp[2]=1, dp[j]=dp[1]=1. Compute dp[j]+1=2. Update dp[2] = max(1, 2) = 2.
Given nums=[1,2,3,4,5], dp=[1,2,3,1,1]. For i=3, j=0: nums[0]=1, nums[3]=4. Check if 1 ≤ 4: yes. Current dp[3]=1, dp[j]=dp[0]=1. Compute dp[j]+1=2. Update dp[3] = max(1, 2) = 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Compare Two Numbers for Non-Decreasing Order
ladders.desc: Check if one number is less than or equal to another.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that in a sorted array or subsequence, each element must be greater than or equal to the previous one to maintain non-decreasing order. This is a basic comparison operation: given two numbers a and b, we check if a ≤ b. If true, b can come after a in the sequence without violating sorted order. This is fundamental because it determines whether we can extend a subsequence. The operation is simple: use the comparison operator <=. Time and space are O(1). This connects to the basic operation by being the condition for updating dp. Common mistakes include using < instead of ≤, which would require strictly increasing order. Real code pattern: if a <= b: # can extend.

examples.trace: Given a=2, b=3. Check if 2 ≤ 3: yes, so True.
Given a=5, b=2. Check if 5 ≤ 2: no, so False.

approaches.name: Greedy with Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain a dynamic array to build the LIS greedily: iterate through the array, using binary search to find the position to replace or append elements to keep the array sorted. The length of this array gives the LIS length, and deletions are calculated as array length minus LIS length.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Deletions to Make Array Sorted Using Greedy LIS with Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution: find minimum deletions by computing longest increasing subsequence length using greedy binary search approach.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that to minimize deletions to make an array sorted, we need to preserve the longest possible increasing subsequence (LIS). The deletions required equal total elements minus LIS length. We compute LIS efficiently using a greedy approach with binary search in O(n log n) time. We maintain a dynamic array 'tails' where tails[i] stores the smallest possible ending value of an increasing subsequence of length i+1. As we iterate through the input array, for each element x, we use binary search to find the position in tails where x can replace an existing element (if x is smaller than some tails[j]) or be appended (if x is larger than all tails). This greedy replacement ensures we maintain the smallest possible ending values, allowing longer subsequences to form later. The final length of tails gives the LIS length. The algorithm works because by always keeping the smallest possible ending values, we maximize opportunities for future elements to extend subsequences. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to n elements each requiring binary search in tails (O(log n)). Space is O(n) for tails array. Connection to deletions: deletions = n - len(tails). Common mistakes include forgetting to handle duplicate values correctly (should replace, not skip), incorrect binary search bounds, or misunderstanding that tails doesn't store the actual LIS but its length. Real pattern: initialize empty tails, iterate nums, binary search for insertion position, update or append, return n - len(tails).

examples.trace: Input: [5,2,8,6,3,6,9,5], n=8. Initialize tails=[]. Process 5: tails empty, append -> tails=[5]. Process 2: binary search in [5] finds position 0 (2<5), replace -> tails=[2]. Process 8: binary search in [2] finds position 1 (8>2), append -> tails=[2,8]. Process 6: binary search in [2,8] finds position 1 (6<8), replace -> tails=[2,6]. Process 3: binary search in [2,6] finds position 1 (3<6), replace -> tails=[2,3]. Process 6: binary search in [2,3] finds position 2 (6>3), append -> tails=[2,3,6]. Process 9: binary search in [2,3,6] finds position 3 (9>6), append -> tails=[2,3,6,9]. Process 5: binary search in [2,3,6,9] finds position 2 (5<6), replace -> tails=[2,3,5,9]. Final tails length=4, deletions=8-4=4. LIS could be [2,3,6,9] or [2,3,5,9].

Input: [1,2,3,4,5], n=5. Initialize tails=[]. Process 1: append -> tails=[1]. Process 2: append -> tails=[1,2]. Process 3: append -> tails=[1,2,3]. Process 4: append -> tails=[1,2,3,4]. Process 5: append -> tails=[1,2,3,4,5]. Final tails length=5, deletions=5-5=0. Array already sorted.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Greedy LIS Length Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Compute length of longest increasing subsequence using greedy binary search method.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes the LIS length without tracking deletions. The intuition is to maintain the smallest possible ending values for increasing subsequences of various lengths, allowing us to extend them greedily. We use an array 'tails' where tails[i] = smallest ending value of an increasing subsequence of length i+1. For each element x in input: 1) If x is greater than all elements in tails, append x (increasing max length). 2) Otherwise, find the smallest index i where tails[i] >= x using binary search, and set tails[i] = x (replacing to keep ending values minimal). This replacement is key: by making tails[i] smaller, future elements have better chance to extend subsequences. The algorithm ensures tails remains sorted, enabling binary search. Time O(n log n): n iterations with O(log k) binary search where k ≤ n. Space O(n) for tails. Connection to full problem: LIS length directly gives deletions when subtracted from n. Common mistakes: using linear search instead of binary search (O(n²)), incorrectly handling equal values (should replace, not skip to maintain non-decreasing tails for strictly increasing LIS). Real pattern: tails=[], for x in nums: pos = bisect_left(tails, x), if pos==len(tails): tails.append(x), else: tails[pos]=x, return len(tails).

examples.trace: Input: [5,2,8,6,3,6,9,5]. tails=[]. x=5: bisect_left([],5)=0, append -> tails=[5]. x=2: bisect_left([5],2)=0, replace -> tails=[2]. x=8: bisect_left([2],8)=1, append -> tails=[2,8]. x=6: bisect_left([2,8],6)=1, replace -> tails=[2,6]. x=3: bisect_left([2,6],3)=1, replace -> tails=[2,3]. x=6: bisect_left([2,3],6)=2, append -> tails=[2,3,6]. x=9: bisect_left([2,3,6],9)=3, append -> tails=[2,3,6,9]. x=5: bisect_left([2,3,6,9],5)=2, replace -> tails=[2,3,5,9]. Length=4.

Input: [5,4,3,2,1]. tails=[]. x=5: append -> tails=[5]. x=4: bisect_left([5],4)=0, replace -> tails=[4]. x=3: bisect_left([4],3)=0, replace -> tails=[3]. x=2: bisect_left([3],2)=0, replace -> tails=[2]. x=1: bisect_left([2],1)=0, replace -> tails=[1]. Length=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Smallest Ending Values with Greedy Replacement
ladders.desc: The central technique: keep array of smallest possible ending values for increasing subsequences, updating greedily.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the greedy replacement logic without binary search implementation. The intuition is that to maximize LIS length, we want ending values of subsequences to be as small as possible, so future elements can extend them more easily. We maintain an array 'tails' that is always sorted. For each new element x: if x is larger than all tails, we start a new longer subsequence by appending x. If x is smaller than some tail, we replace the first tail that is ≥ x with x. This replacement doesn't change the length of existing subsequences but makes their endings smaller, potentially allowing longer subsequences later. Example: with tails=[2,5] and x=3, we replace 5 with 3 to get [2,3]; though we lose the subsequence ending with 5, we keep one ending with 3 which is better for future elements like 4. The algorithm ensures tails[i] is the minimum possible value that can end an increasing subsequence of length i+1. This greedy choice is optimal because any alternative keeping larger ending values would not yield longer subsequences. Time O(n²) if using linear search, but optimized with binary search in higher level. Space O(n). Connection to L1: this logic is the heart of the LIS computation. Common mistakes: not replacing when x equals a tail (should replace to handle non-decreasing sequences correctly), misunderstanding that tails doesn't store actual subsequences. Real pattern: for x in nums: find position i where tails[i] >= x (linear scan here), if i found: tails[i]=x, else: tails.append(x).

examples.trace: Input: [2,5,3,7]. Initialize tails=[]. Process 2: larger than all tails? yes (empty), append -> tails=[2]. Process 5: larger than all tails? yes (2<5), append -> tails=[2,5]. Process 3: larger than all tails? no (3<5). Find first tail ≥3: tails[1]=5≥3, replace -> tails=[2,3]. Process 7: larger than all tails? yes (3<7), append -> tails=[2,3,7]. Final tails=[2,3,7], length=3.

Input: [4,1,3,2]. tails=[]. Process 4: append -> tails=[4]. Process 1: larger than all tails? no (1<4). Find first tail ≥1: tails[0]=4≥1, replace -> tails=[1]. Process 3: larger than all tails? yes (1<3), append -> tails=[1,3]. Process 2: larger than all tails? no (2<3). Find first tail ≥2: tails[1]=3≥2, replace -> tails=[1,2]. Final tails=[1,2], length=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Find insertion position of element in sorted array using binary search.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is binary search on a sorted array to find the position where an element should be inserted to maintain sorted order. Specifically, we use bisect_left: given sorted array arr and value x, return the leftmost index i such that arr[i] >= x. If x is greater than all elements, return len(arr). The intuition is to repeatedly divide the search interval in half, comparing x to the middle element, until the correct position is found. This is efficient O(log n) vs linear scan O(n). Steps: initialize low=0, high=len(arr). While low < high: mid = (low+high)//2, if arr[mid] < x: low = mid+1, else: high = mid. Return low. This finds the first position where arr[i] >= x. For LIS, we use this to find where to replace or append in tails array. Time O(log n), space O(1). Connection to L2: binary search optimizes finding the replacement position in tails. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in bounds, incorrect comparison for bisect_left (use < for moving low), not handling empty array. Real pattern: function bisect_left(arr, x): low, high = 0, len(arr); while low < high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] < x: low = mid+1; else: high = mid; return low.

examples.trace: arr=[2,5,8], x=6. low=0, high=3. Iter1: mid=(0+3)//2=1, arr[1]=5 < 6? yes, low=2. low=2, high=3. Iter2: mid=(2+3)//2=2, arr[2]=8 < 6? no, high=2. low=2, high=2, exit. Return 2. Position 2 is where 6 should be inserted (between 5 and 8).

arr=[1,3,5], x=0. low=0, high=3. Iter1: mid=(0+3)//2=1, arr[1]=3 < 0? no, high=1. low=0, high=1. Iter2: mid=(0+1)//2=0, arr[0]=1 < 0? no, high=0. low=0, high=0, exit. Return 0. Position 0 is where 0 should be inserted (before 1).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Array and Updating Dynamic Array
ladders.desc: Fundamental loop structure: traverse array and conditionally modify another array.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating through an input array and performing conditional updates on a separate dynamic array. The intuition is simple sequential processing: for each element in the input, check a condition and then either append to or replace an element in the dynamic array. This is a foundational programming pattern. Steps: 1) Initialize an empty dynamic array. 2) Loop through each element x in input array. 3) For each x, determine if it should be appended (if it meets a condition like being larger than all current elements) or replace an existing element (if it meets another condition). 4) Update the dynamic array accordingly. In the context of LIS, the condition involves comparing x to elements in the dynamic array, but at this level we abstract that to a generic check. Time O(n) for the loop, plus O(k) for checks if done linearly. Space O(n) for dynamic array. Connection to L3: this loop structure is where binary search is inserted to optimize checks. Common mistakes: not initializing the dynamic array, off-by-one in loop indices, incorrectly updating array in-place. Real pattern: dynamic_arr = []; for x in input_arr: if condition_append(x, dynamic_arr): dynamic_arr.append(x); else: idx = find_position(x, dynamic_arr); dynamic_arr[idx] = x.

examples.input: input=[3,1,4], append if x>max(dynamic), else replace first >=x
examples.trace: Input: [3,1,4]. dynamic=[]. x=3: condition append? max([]) undefined, treat as append -> dynamic=[3]. x=1: condition append? max([3])=3, 1>3? no. Find first >=1: dynamic[0]=3≥1, replace -> dynamic=[1]. x=4: condition append? max([1])=1, 4>1? yes, append -> dynamic=[1,4]. Final dynamic=[1,4].

examples.input: input=[5,2,5,3], append if x>last(dynamic), else replace first >=x
examples.trace: Input: [5,2,5,3]. dynamic=[]. x=5: append (empty) -> dynamic=[5]. x=2: append? last=5, 2>5? no. Find first >=2: dynamic[0]=5≥2, replace -> dynamic=[2]. x=5: append? last=2, 5>2? yes, append -> dynamic=[2,5]. x=3: append? last=5, 3>5? no. Find first >=3: dynamic[1]=5≥3, replace -> dynamic=[2,3]. Final dynamic=[2,3].

testCases.input: input=[3,1,4], append if x>max(dynamic), else replace first >=x
input=[5,2,5,3], append if x>last(dynamic), else replace first >=x
input=[1,2,3], append if x>0, else replace
input=[4,4,4], append if x>3, else replace
input=[7,1,9], append if x>5, else replace first
input=[2,8,4], append if x>dynamic_length, else replace at 0
input=[6,3,5], append if x even, else replace at 0
input=[5,1,2], append if x>1, else replace at 0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Comparisons
ladders.desc: Prerequisite: understand arrays, indexing, and simple value comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers basic array operations and comparisons, essential for beginners. An array is an ordered collection of elements accessible by index (starting at 0). Operations include: creating an array, accessing elements via index, modifying elements, appending new elements, and finding array length. Comparisons involve comparing two values using operators: < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal), == (equal). For example, given array arr = [3,1,4], arr[0] is 3, arr[1] is 1, arr[2] is 4; length is 3. To append 2: arr becomes [3,1,4,2]. To compare: 3 < 4 is true, 1 == 1 is true. In the context of LIS, we use arrays to store sequences and comparisons to check if one element is larger than another to maintain increasing order. Time O(1) for access/modify, O(1) amortized for append, O(1) for comparison. Space O(n) for array storage. Connection to L4: these basics are used in the loop to traverse and update arrays. Common mistakes: index out of bounds (e.g., accessing arr[3] in length-3 array), confusing array indices, incorrect comparison logic. Real pattern: arr = []; arr.append(5); if arr[0] < 10: arr[0] = 7.

examples.trace: Start with arr=[5,2,8]. Compare 5 and 2: 5 < 2? false. Append 6: arr becomes [5,2,8,6]. Access arr[0]=5, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=8, arr[3]=6. Length is 4.

examples.input: Array [1], compare 1 and 3, modify index 0 to 4
examples.trace: Start with arr=[1]. Compare 1 and 3: 1 < 3? true. Modify arr[0] to 4: arr becomes [4]. Access arr[0]=4. Length is 1.

testCases.input: Array [1], compare 1 and 3, modify index 0 to 4
